comments.title: Symbel, Meditation, a Request
comments.content:
Good evening, and a happy early solstice to you and all here!

1) This one is as much for the commentariat as JMG: In honor of the solstice, and because I got the idea for how to challenge myself from JMG, I would like to ask the help of any here willing in a virtual version of the heathen ritual of symbel. At important ritual occasions, heathens pass around a blessed drink (usually mead or ale), and as each person gets the horn, he or she makes a toast or a boast. "Boasts" can be of something accomplished, or of something intended for the coming year. Despite the modern connotation of the word, arrogance or excessive pride in boasts are poor form. They're meant to be a way to strengthen the individual's commitment to acting rightly by creating public accountability with Gods and Men for what you will do, and to get some public recognition in front of the same when you succeed. In my case, I'm planning to challenge myself to read only books written by dead people in 2022, with a handful of potential exceptions (if work somehow requires a book read, if I'm learning a skill I will immediately put to use, or if a family member or close friend writes something).

So, if you will, join me in a toast (with whatever beverage suits you), or else send good energy in whatever way you're comfortable with, to my following boast, I'd be much obliged (kindly forgive this early stumbling go at Germanic alliterative verse, I'm pretty new to this):

"Hail Woden, high king,
wide-faring God of Wisdom,
back me in this boast:
Only books by those unliving
will I read while this year turns,
unless needed by unbending work,
or teaching a craft taken on soon after,
or those called up by kith and kin.
Let all here who love learning,
and forbearers of free-thinking fond
witness my bold words
and wish well my work!"

A couple of questions on discursive meditation, but first, thanks very much for your suggestions from Way of the Golden Section to include a standard prayer at the start of each meditation and a spontaneous one at the end - I have found those have already added much to the practice. As for questions:

2) First: What led you to not include color breathing and/or the cleansing breath in the chapter on discursive meditation in The Way of the Golden Section?

3) Related: in the Dreamwidth posts on Discursive Meditation, you suggest a few cycles of Four-Fold Breath at the end of a meditation, but that instruction is not included in either the Druidry Handbook or The Way of the Golden Section. Any thoughts on the pros/cons of ending with some amount of the Four-Fold Breath besides "experiment and see what works for you"?

4) Not so much a question as a longer-term request for consideration: I read After Progress this week and found it very helpful, even if most of the ideas were familiar from the blog. It struck me that there were some complimentary ideas to World Full of Gods, but that you were soft-pedaling the spiritual/occult dimension to make a more palatable case for folks of a materialist bent. So, I, for one, would love to see a work of theology/occult philosophy from you that integrates your current thinking on religion, the theosphere, the subtle planes, and so forth, but "with the gloves off" (i.e. with as frank a discussion of the occult side of things as you're willing to include). So, with all of the spare time in your writing schedule, if you could just knock out that tiny little undertaking, I'm sure I'd not the only one that would be grateful :)

Thank you again for providing this forum and sharing your knowledge, it has been truly valuable.

Cheers,
Jeff

Recent DNA studies suggest some Neanderthals may have had red hair so apparently aliens have been visiting us for quite a while. However, for all his faults, Carl Sagan pointed out the impossibility of interbreeding with aliens, saying we'd have better luck interbreeding with petunias because they are actually more closely related to us than any alien would be.

The confusion between gods and aliens keeps cropping up. The Annunnaki (ancient Middle Eastern gods) are a good example and I've seen attempts to explain legendary Irish weapons such as the Spear of Lugh as being alien technology. This conflation of divinities and speculation about life on other worlds suggests people raised in a secular environment don't really understand the difference.

Some might make the case that any technology advanced enough would be indistinguishable from magic and presumably its creators would be indistinguishable from gods, that somehow aliens are more 'evolved' than us and so just seem like gods. But I think that trivializes the divine. JMG has pointed out technology has hard physical limits that we can't exceed no matter how hard we try. That would be just as true for aliens as it would be for us. As physical beings they are subject to the laws of physics just like us poor Earthly schmucks.

I wonder how Christians would react if someone tried to portray Jesus as a half-alien being with psychic powers rather than the Son of God. It would no doubt infuriate them and bring out accusations of blasphemy, which might explain why many of these speculations are generally confined to pantheons which most people no longer worship. In reducing the divinities to mere concrete flesh and blood (or the equivalent) you risk losing the awe and respect one should always accord to that which is greater than yourself. The gods become just another bunch of Star Trekish characters living over in the next solar system.

JLfromNH/Maroon Barmy Porpoise

A poster responding to another topic on this blog recently remarked that the condition of vertigo is related to depletion of or damage to the etheric body. Since you did not rebut this assertion, I am guessing it may well be the case.

Is there any magical practice or operation that can help to heal a depleted, unbalanced or otherwise damaged etheric body? If so, I would like to do this for my sister, with her informed consent of course.

My background is in GD, working with Hellenic deities. I use Tarot for divination.

comments.title: Figuring out the difference between external and purely personal phenomena
comments.content: I like to think my main goal in life is self-knowledge, but I also like to feel empowered to accomplish what I want in life. Both of these motivations feed into my recently-begun magic practice. I worry about failing to see where the inner world ends and the outer begins, e.g., misapprehending imaginative psychic responses to a difficult emotion as a malevolent sprit. I really want to avoid this kind of pitfall, which could threaten both empowering and understanding myself. Saturn (tightly conjunct Venus, ouch) is square Neptune in my birth horoscope, so I struggle with illusion. I have meditated and done divination with this problem in mind and will continue to do so; one of my thoughts was that the boundary only becomes absolutely clear at death, when inputs from the gross body cease. What advice do you have for not succumbing to self-deception in magic?

replies.title: Re: Figuring out the difference between external and purely personal phenomena
replies.content: The main risks of self-deception in magical training come early on, before you have enough experience to deal with the complex boundary between the worlds. The crucial rule is to remember that for all practical purposes, everything you'll experience for the first decade or so of your magical work will be purely psychological in nature, and what isn't psychological can be treated as though it is -- for example, the banishing ritual you should be practicing daily will banish hostile spirits even if you think of those spirits as psychological phenomena. So assume that it's personal for the time being, and you should be fine.

replies.title: Re: Figuring out the difference between external and purely personal phenomena
replies.content: Not the OP: that's really interesting! And I guess that's why Dion Fortune, somewhere in Sane Occultism, recommends that the occultist have a sound footing in psychology, or at least a good general knowledge of it.

replies.title: Re: Figuring out the difference between external and purely personal phenomena
replies.content: Exactly. Too much of what passes for psychology these days is the opposite of helpful, but reading some Jung and some books on Transactional Analysis certainly helped me.

replies.title: Re: Figuring out the difference between external and purely personal phenomena
replies.content:
JMG's answer made a great deal of sense given my own personal experience.

So far in my own journey I've found myself not being too concerned with this boundary. Perhaps the best way to deal with your worry about self-deception is to assume you cannot know where this boundary is and accept that limitation.

In my experience, your thoughts are still your thoughts, the ultimate cause is less important. My occult practice so far has allowed me more insight into my own thoughts, so I'm able to better see my unhelpful thought patterns. I probe the historic roots of these by going to therapy. IMO, no matter what the cause, the way to deal with them seems to be the same: gain more insight into your own mind, and try to apply your will to your most unhelpful thought patterns.

All of this of course is so much easier said than done! Just because I'm able to articulate this doesn't mean I'm any good at successfully implementing it yet :)

replies.content:
If I may offer my unsolicited opinion.

Following the binary, in its reciprocal form, I can tell you exactly where you draw the line. If you want to predict what happens in X years, you have to first predict what will happen in half of that, right? Whenever you stop, you have your answer. So, If you want to predict what happens in 10 years, then, first you need to tell me what will happen in 5 years. Then, if you want to predict what happens in 5 years, you have to tell me what happens in 2.5 years ... if you want to predict what happens in 2.5 femto seconds, you have to tell me first what will happen in 1.25 femto seconds, and so on. Do you see the problem with that approach?

You can calculate the trajectory of a stone with the well known equation of a parabola, right? However, people that launch missiles into targets know that it is just a model to understand reality, not a hard truth that determines what happens in the real world. Ballistic systems have to be updated every millisecond to adjust for changes in the speed of the wind, temperature, etc, even though the average trajectory of a missile can still be determined in average by an equation.

The fact that you know what is the form of the path launched objects take doesn't mean, changing analogies a little bit, that if you know who is throwing a stone, with what force and from what place, it is guaranteed that it will fall where you predicted even if it has followed it half of the way. Who knows? A bird might get in the way, another stone can be thrown to intercept the other stone and a myriad of other situations can happen that alter the prediction given the initial conditions that set the events in motion.

Deterministic models are good to understand statistical averages but for individual events, and also to make sense of some events, in physics, but when you zoom in, you have to do a lot of work to actually make something land where you want it to. It is a means, not an end. For example, remember when the atom used to be a "Plum pudding" atom? In a way, Thomson was right when he said that, because it explained some observations, however, now we have the Heisenberg uncertainty principle which speaks about a dischordian, or chaotic mass of particles that statistically can be predicted but when you try to actually find a particle, determinism breaks down and things get weird.

Hail Eris! Gobble, gobble, gobble, gobble, gobble

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Don't think in terms of single deterministic trajectories; think in terms of probability distributions over trajectories. Sometimes the current situation has details that concentrate the probability of some aspects of the trajectory in ways that make them mostly predictable.

The discussion in the current Ecosophia main post references Judea Pearl. What Pearl did was clarify how to sort out and organize some intuitive thoughts about how probability works in these kinds of situations. If your ballistic projectile landed left of where you expected, in what proportions is that because the wind is blowing left, or because your sight is calibrated wrong, or because that's how the speed-dependent turbulence around the projectile worked this time, or because that's how the chaotic random forces during launch worked this time? What if the wind isn't consistent? These will all have different implications for future launches, and you can use Pearl's insights (in the form of a "dynamic Bayesian network") to sort out which pressures of evidence from the observed trajectories or landing points should push which ways on which beliefs about the different combinations of explanations.

(Also, David BTL, if you see this: if you only have a forecasting method for future energy demand that makes a single point-estimate trajectory, you should look for something better that at least lets you randomly "roll out" multiple sampled trajectories with different random perturbations. One way to tell when a model has been using its own predictions for too long starts with the idea of a "Lyapunov exponent", but that comes from the deterministic-trajectory way of thinking about the future. The kinds of problems I run into with models are less about the state of the world and more about the how we even make the state definable in the first place; they look less like "the Lyapunov exponent is too large given the timescale" and more like "Knightian uncertainty" and "social construction".)

The predictability horizon timescales associated with Lyapunov exponents and Markov mixing eigenvalues naturally vary with the details of the system under consideration, and with the choice of what part of the system you focus on versus what part you treat as a source of random noise inputs. In natural forecasting, the transition from predictable to unpredictable is smooth. Think of what happens to the log-odds that a number drawn from a Gaussian distribution will stay within a bin that extends to some given distance from the distribution mean, as you take the distribution variance from some small finite value to some large finite value.

comments.content:
Hi!

In many nature based religions the solstices and equinoxes are important dates in the calendar. I am also thinking of the many alignments found in ancient monuments. It must have been painstaking to accurately track the heavens and build accordingly.

Beyond agricultural uses and timing of the seasons, why would ancient people spend so much time and effort in tracking the solstices, equinoxes and precessions with great precision?

Many regards and thanks to all!

replies.title: Re: December Solstice
replies.content:
Good heavens, yes. The solstices and equinoxes are extremely important in occultism. At each equinox, when the Sun crosses the celestial equator, there's a surge of astral and etheric energy from the Sun to the Earth; if you know how, you can tap into that flow to charge your own subtle bodies and the subtle body of, say, a magical lodge. At each solstice, the energy that flowed in at the previous equinox finishes percolating through the subtle bodies of the Earth, and there's a little "hang time" before it begins fading out; if you know how, you can use that moment of stillness (it's actually about 96 hours long) to bring your subtle bodies and that of a magical lodge into balance.

Many religions use those times, or times derived from them, for special ritual work. Consider Easter -- the first Sunday after the first full moon on or after the spring equinox. Thus you've got the aftermath of the solar influx at the equinox and the early stages of a waning moon -- which is what you want, of course, if your religion works mostly with the solar current and follows an ascetic-Apollonian rather than an ecstatic-Dionysian path.

replies.title: Re: December Solstice
replies.content: So, Dionysians might similarly avail themselves of the first new moon after the fall equinox?

replies.title: Re: December Solstice
replies.content: Yes, exactly. For a Dionysian rite you'd want the solar current waning and the Moon waxing.

replies.title: Re: December Solstice
replies.content: So, would the first Monday after the first new moon after the fall equinox be the ideal for a Dionysian rite?

replies.title: Re: December Solstice
replies.content: That or the first Friday!

replies.title: Re: December Solstice
replies.content: And, to extend this further, would the ideal time would be sunset?

replies.title: Re: December Solstice
replies.content: Yes, or you could have an initial rite at noon, spend the afternoon getting ready, and then have the dancing start as the sun goes down and continue until dawn.

comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I'm writing on a topic I'm curious if you've heard anything about. People often say that modern Western societies lack the kinds of formalized magical practices that exist in other cultures. However, I'm beginning to suspect that an order of magical practitioners have been hiding in plain sight in the modern West:

Clowns.

Many people don't know this, but clowning is an art form with a long tradition and a structured system of roles, rituals and symbols. The modern Western clowning tradition has a direct lineage that can be traced back at least to the 16th-century Italian commedia del'arte; some say that the Italian performers have roots going all the way back to the Greek theater and before, but I don't know of definitive evidence. The earliest records of clowns were in the courts of Egyptian pharaohs.

Clowns express themselves through a language of stock characters and scenarios to guide their audience through specific emotional states. The clown is often a vessel for thoughts and feelings that can't be openly expressed, and experienced, classically-trained clowns I've spoken to have a deep understanding of Jungian psychology and ceremonial magic - although they don't call them by those names! Have you heard anything about connections between clowning and the occult? I haven't been able to find much, aside from a silly book called Clown Occultism that purportedly teaches you to summon the spirits of famous clowns.

Of course, there are well-known links between the esoteric and the theater in all its forms, but the difference I see with clowns is the structured, intentional nature of their approach. A true clown definitely has an idea what's going on when they establish a ritual space and conjure spirits, although they don't use the language we do to describe the acts.

My second question is this: in recent years, the clown has become an increasingly negative figure in popular culture. Clowns have always had light and dark sides, but the mainstream perception of clowns now emphasizes only the latter. Clowns are murderers (John Wayne Gacy, American Horror Story), monsters (Stephen King's IT) and harbingers of doom (the 2016 "clown scare"). Do you have any thoughts as to why these figures of fun have recently been framed in such a negative way?

My hypothesis, echoing things you've said before, is that our societies increasingly lack confidence in their official narratives and that a figure who embodies the unspoken and unacknowledged threatens their dwindling sense of security. It's notable that Napoleon banned the commedia del'arte in Europe and it didn't re-emerge in Italy until the 1970s. It's also notable that anti-establishment thinkers of late talk a lot about "clown world" and evoke images of clowns to critique aspects of society they find absurd.

Thanks for your time.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Okay, let's take this a bit at a time.

1) No one book is "the source" of Hermetic philosophy. Hermetic philosophy is a broad, rich, diverse tradition that draws from many sources. The Emerald Tablet is a good introductory text of alchemical theory, and well worth studying, but it's not the be-all and end-all of Hermeticism.

2) The Emerald Tablet isn't Egyptian in origin. Its first appearance is in an 8th-century Arabic alchemical text, and it was probably first composed in Greek sometime between the 1st and 8th centuries AD. Trace its ideas back far enough and you get to the mingling of Greek, Egyptian, and Babylonian ideas in the eastern Mediterranean after Alexander the Great's time, so there's an Egyptian connection, but it's nuanced and complex.

3) The whole line of cant that runs "a stole it from b who stole it from c" is a typical piece of modern grievance rhetoric, dripping with historical ignorance. Anyone who uses that sort of argument ought to be forced to do all their future computer work on a Datapoint 2200, with 2k of memory and an 8 inch floppy drive --
[IMAGE:0]
-- after all, that was the original personal computer, and all later PCs stole the idea from them!

Ancient Egypt had a rich and glorious tradition of magic; so did ancient Mesopotamia, and so did ancient Greece. Those flowed together and combined with other streams of magical practice in ancient times to produce the splendid magical syntheses of the late Roman world. Those were obscured for a time during the early Middle Ages, but were revived in the later Middle Ages and flowered again, drawing in yet more ideas and creative effort, in the Renaissance; obscured again by the rise of rationalism, the magical tradition proved irrepressible and broke free again in the 1850s, and we're still riding that wave.

replies.title: Re: Planetary charity candles

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: JMG - I'm curious to understand your perspective on Christianity.  I have been a Christian (fairly traditional American style) most of my life, from long before I started studying magic and the occult.  I understand that they are compatible, but I'm having a hard time reconciling where Christianity fits in to the big picture.  For instance, how to reconcile the idea of salvation and heaven in Christianity within a broader context of reincarnation.  Also, the Bible describes God as creator of all things, which is quite different than just being one of many gods.  Those are just a couple questions, but I'm looking for a good resource to put it all in perspective.  Thanks!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
You wrote: "the Bible describes God as creator of all things, which is quite different than just being one of many gods."

Actually, that's Christian theology speaking here, not the Bible. There are many, many hints in the Old Testament that other Gods also exist, but allegiance to them is forbidden to the descendants of Abraham.

Many decades ago, Otter/Oberon Zell wrote a short essay ("We Are the Other People") to the effect that the creation story in Genesis 1 and the second creation story in Genesis 2 are not two accounts of the same event, but accounts of two different events that happened one after the other. In Genesis 1, it is the Elohim (a plural in Hebrew) that created humankind in the way described. In Genesis 2, one of the Elohim, namely YHWH, went off and created Adam and Eve in a Garden he had established for his own purposes. Must of the rest of the Torah is about the descendants of Adam and Eve, but the world in which these descendants dwell is populated by other humans as well, not descended form Adam and Eve, who are mentioned here and there in passing (Cain's wife for starters). You might enjoy reading it for its own sake, and it may also help resolve your perplexity. (You can find it in many places on the web.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Keep in mind that I'm not a Christian. I haven't even been baptized, and I never went to a church service until I was in my late teens. (I thought it was odd and rather off-putting.) So my perspective on Christianity is by definition an outsider's perspective.

That said...

There's a difference between having a relationship with a god and believing a set of propositions about that god. I've known quite a few people who have very strong personal relationships with Jesus but who do not believe in standard Christian theology. Thus it's quite possible to be a Christian, in the sense of having faith in Christ, without believing standard claims about how many gods there are and what happens when you die.

Keep in mind that Jesus said, "Thou shalt love the Lord thy God with all thy heart, and with all thy soul, and with all thy mind. This is the first and great commandment. And the second is like unto it, Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself." He did not say, "Thou shalt accept without question the following set of claims about theology," and go on from there! For that matter, the First Commandment in Exodus doesn't say "there are no other gods," it just says "thou shalt have no other gods before Me."

Christian occultists are a diverse lot. Some of them accept monotheism, others see Christ as one divine power among many. Some of them believe in reincarnation, others hold to a more standard view, and then you get still other beliefs! I would encourage you to read some books by Christian occultists -- Gareth Knight's Experience of the Inner Worlds is a good starting point, and (despite its politically incorrect bits) Max Heindel's The Rosicrucian Cosmo-Conception might be a good second volume to read. Those will give you some perspective to start with.

replies.title: Re: Music, Etheric Gods, Asuras
replies.content:
Not exactly answering your question 3), but trying to provide some additional ways you might sneak up on it.

First, one link between the Zoroastrian and Hindu/Buddhist Asuras and Devas is one of shared cultural and linguistic heritage, as it's pretty well accepted that the modern Indic and Persian languages are descended from a language that split off from Proto-Indo-European and was its own thing for a while. If you'd like an extremely thorough exploration of what we think we can know about the people who spoke Proto-Indo-European and where they lived, The Horse, the Wheel, and Language is a fantastic inter-disciplinary work. Since it is written by an archaeologist, unless you get really excited by pottery sherds, you might find stretches rather dry, but if you can get through that stuff, it gives a very complete picture of the evidence for the origins of the people who spoke Proto-Indo-European and when various groups likely split off. Unfortunately, for most of the language groups I've looked at (I've been most interested in Germanic languages), there is very little coverage between the split from Proto-Indo-European and when speakers of that language ran into folks with writing who talked about them and/or they began writing themselves. Having both ends allows you to make at least a few inferences about the time in between, though, so hopefully this will be helpful in giving you ideas about what you might look into.

The second thing I'd like to mention goes a bit more into Indo-European comparative mythology. One of the influential scholars in the field, Georges Dumezil, proposed that Proto-Indo-European society was divided into 3 functions: priest/wizards, warriors, and producers (farmers and craftsmen). There are myths in many Indo-European languages about Gods and Goddesses that might represent these functions going to war and (sometimes) coming to some kind of negotiated peace. Dumezil proposes that it was the Priest/Wizards and Warriors on one side and the Producers on the other. In Norse myth, that would be the Aesir on the one hand (typically more associated with the sky) and the Vanir on the other (typically more associated with the earth and fertility). The asura/deva split among Indo-Iranian language and myth is sometimes said to be an echo of that, though with less of a clear link to the functions in the version of asuras and devas that Zoroastrianism, Hinduism, and Buddhism talk about. These myths may or may have been linked with a historical event where two groups of people with different ways of life fought and eventually merged into a single people, but that's not strictly necessary for it to be potentially relevant for considering different kinds of spiritual beings.

Anyhow, I hope one or both of those might prove to be useful avenues to look into, and if you'd like to geek out about anything to do with the Proto-Indo-Europeans and comparative mythology, please let me know!

Disclaimer: If you do any digging at all into the origins of the Indo-European language family and what we might know about the people who spoke the language ancestral to that language family, you are going to bump into potential controversies and the contortions they have resulted in. Short version: Nazis and some of their immediate predecessors believed that the Proto-Indo-European language originated in Scandinavia, was 100% linked with the race of the people speaking it, and that the Germanic languages and people were the purest remnant of this origin. Later scholars distanced themselves from all of this in various ways, and everyone involved is careful to be very precise about what they do and do not have evidence for, which sometimes leads to folks not quite coming out and saying things that would normally be reasonable inferences. For example, some linguists refuse to engage with the idea that we can say anything meaningful about the religion or culture of the speakers of Proto-Indo-European, despite linguistic cognates, striking similarities of belief and practice between widely-spaced speakers of descendent languages, and so forth.

replies.title: Re: Geomantic reading

comments.title: Telluric currents
comments.content:
It seems to me that there is a rich history and tradition of practices related to heavenly influences streaming down to us, such as astrology, but very few related to telluric, earthly influences bubbling up to us.

Why might this be?

What practices would you recommend starting with to begin sensing and working with telluric currents?

replies.title: Re: Telluric currents
replies.content:
1) Because for the last two thousand years our culture has been dominated by a religious sensibility that thought that the earth was at best a vale of tears and at worst a snare set by Satan to catch our immortal souls. That kind of attitude doesn't support the survival of telluric traditions.

2) Spend at least fifteen minutes a week outdoors, in a place that has growing things in it and a good view of the sky. Pay attention to what happens around you. Don't distract yourself from the environment with any of the internal or external sources of chatter people these days like to use; just attend to the natural world. It's by attuning to nature that you can begin to sense the telluric influence.

comments.title: Renaissance Melancholy
comments.content:
JMG,

I’m really enjoying learning Geomancy with your book and I wonder, what else goes together with it? As in, the Renaissance context… Is your Sacred Geometry part of this?

Thanks for Magic Mondays

replies.title: Re: Renaissance Melancholy
replies.content:
No, it's not necessarily Christian. Renaissance magic was poised between Christianity and classical Greco-Roman religion, and could go either way. The complete system of Renaissance occultism included astrology, astrological magic, herbal medicine, astrological gardening, alchemy (herbal, metallic, and spiritual), Neoplatonic philosophy, Pythagorean numerology, sacred geometry, occult musical theory, and swordsmanship.

My book of essays The City of Hermes is basically about that subject -- all the papers included in it were written at a time when I was trying to revive Renaissance Hermetic occultism as a live spiritual option. The short form was that nobody else was interested, and then I got handed a Druid order to revive and my attention went in other directions for a while...

replies.title: Re: Fasting talisman
replies.content: I would be very, very, very careful with this. By making such a talisman, you could quite easily give yourself a fatal case of anorexia nervosa. Leave it as an exercise of the will, and do it with the basic rules of will training in mind: that is, start with short and simple exercises you know you can accomplish successfully, establish the habit of success, and build step by step from there.

comments.title: On the elements in the ritual of the pentagram
comments.content:
Hi John
I was having a conversation recently with a friend of mine who is not very familiar with ritual magic, generally about the elemental principles. I was mentioning how with the pentagram ritual you can bring the four elements into balance with the lesser version (by summarizing them through the element of earth), or you can work with particular elements with the greater version, or work with all of them more intensely with the Supreme version. This brought up the topic of elemental permutations, how the court cards of the tarot represent "the x of y" elements, and such. She then asked me "Ok, so the supreme version deals with 'everything of everything'?" (meaning, all 16 elemental permutations). That made me realize that I am not sure wether it is more correct to say so, or to say it deals only with the "pure" versions of the elements, the Fire of Fire, the Air of Air, etc. Some light meditation on it had me thinking her intuition was more correct than I first thought, because one traces the complete pentagrams all around, and even if starting on a particular point renders a pentagram to be of a particular element, you are still formulating them all whole, and in that completion are the other sub-elements implied. What do you think?

Also, happy solstice to you and all here! And whatever other holidays you celebrate in these times, as well as a happy 2022 :)

JP

replies.title: Re: On the elements in the ritual of the pentagram
replies.content:
The supreme pentagram rituals include all the sub-elements. That's why it's so helpful to scry the tattwas or do elemental pathworkings while you're learning and practicing the more advanced pentagram rituals, so you can get a clear sense of each sub-element.

Thank you, and a happy solstice etc. to you and yours.

To (Anonymous): 2021-12-20 10:00 pm (UTC)


I don't know the specifics of your particular situation but perhaps you might find something useful in the following I looked up. The following is from Sri Rohit Arya's The Sacred India Tarot deck and book set.

For the Judgment card he has the following information when it's in the reversed position - p. 124


Shadow: Old problems, Old loves, old nuisances from your past surface, demanding their pound of flesh. If the actions, and indeed the personality, are not based on a higher purpose or calling, you could be in serious trouble. Legal and social problems fall pell-mell on you. All the times you were censorious and judgemental with others, come back to haunt you. Stubborn clinging to a course of action or viewpoint that has caused nothing but distress. Combine that with being mired in habit and repetition and no wonder this card feels like hell. Falling away from spirituality and God because life has treated you 'unfairly'. The card was described by a wise person as 'The Atheist Maker' - not entirely in jest. The Advaita sage Ramesh Balsekar has a pertinent remark about such times, "Our prayer should be one of gratitude. We should thank God for our suffering knowing it could have been much worse, like the millions who live below the poverty line." (the preceding quote is set in italics in the book itself, not just by me).

Special meaning: In other difficult cards there may be extenuating circumstances and excuses for what you are experiencing. In this case, what transpires is exactly and precisely the measure of justice and/or reward you deserve from your karma and there is no appeal against it.

Karma type/ All/None
Karma quality: All/None


Insights of this card:

This is a significant stage of life, a genuine rite of passage, and nothing that is valuable comes without trouble.


******end of transcript of p. 124*****



I know the above sounds pretty bad. I've had readings that have the Judgment reversed card as well.

From the point of view of Shakti (set aside Will for the moment) something about the Shakti-energies of your situation is trying to balance out. Sri Arya gives clues as to how to approach it. The Personality (the egoic-'you') will probably not like the actions of such balancing out. However other gurus I've listened to...including Sri Arya himself say that these kind of situations will sometimes pop up in EVERYBODY'S life - rather difficult karma. Nobody alive has a life where this exact type of karma doesn't pop up sooner or later. In such times your own approach to dealing with it can help you get through. That's why he's saying approaching the situation with higher wisdom can make a difference in not re-sowing the seed and instead it successfully dissipates.

Also (assume for the sake of argument a worst-case-scenario - say...you lose your job or something like that...)

Sri Arya says 'hard-karma' typically runs approximately 18 months to clear itself out but that once its run its course the karma that comes AFTER that is MUCH better than what would have otherwise been the case if you'd limped along with this 'hard karma' overhang unspent.

It's the kind of situation Karl Jung would tell people to break out a bottle of Champaign during its run because although the karma is hard what follows it is loaded with potential for very good things afterwards. Again, by following your Higher Self's "Dao" the chances of very beneficial things to come your way when its all over are much improved.

One extra comment to (Anonymous): 2021-12-20 10:00 pm (UTC).

It's entirely possible that the Shakti the Judgment R card is referring to could be something as simple as your kitten situation. Really all the Judgment R card is signalling is that the energy is getting close to manifesting some kind of balancing action. The question is whether it will re-sow (eventually) or dissipate (eventually). But just because it's Judgment R doesn't mean it's ipso facto dramatically bad. Yes, I know Sri Arya makes it sound like its the dramatically bad kind and sometimes it is but really that's because nobody consults tarot (usually) for smaller shakti-balancing actions about to manifest. comments.title: Tarot Reading on President Biden
comments.content:
I offer the following I did today on Pres. Biden - especially since I was curious about the failing of the BBB legislation going forward.


Curious to hear other's opinions - in particular if they pick up on things I overlooked or are contrary to my own interpretation.


*******************
Joe Biden today on Monday 12/20/2021

I used a 5 card spread for the President. I wanted to see what the energies of the President are that are manifesting today. I use reversals because my divinations are more accurate and finer-grained with them than relying solely on placement to give the attunements. Some of the analysis is my first attempts to use Sri Rohit Arya's investigations into the karmic qualities and karmic types each card tends toward. A lot of the noted readings are drawn directly from Sri Arya's book.

1. Heart of the Matter:

5 of Cups R

2 ways this can be interpretted - positively or negatively given the surrounding cards. The later cards in the reading made it clear it's the latter of the two. That is, this reversal is an intensification of the usual upright negative connotation.

Reading:

The intensification gives:

Shattering or devastating loss, divorce, flawed partnerships, arguments, turmoil from unexpected sources, heartbreak, friendships that persist due to habitual arrangements, regrets

Karma types: Parabdha (karmic consequences due to the way one's cultivated one's self [typically due to habitual ways of thinking or doing things]) and Purushartha (specific current karmic actions taken due to one's free choices in the current situation).

Karma Quality of this card: Dridha - in astrological terms: Fixed, unmovable, inescapable


I see this as most likely referring to Biden's internal reaction (cups - card of water element and of emotions) to the news a fellow Dem has pole-axed his Build Back Better legislative agenda.

The karmic implications of this card is that Biden is the wrong person in the wrong place at the wrong time in order for the the karmic ambitions of the BBB getting passed (treating BBB as a separate kind of Shakti-power that wants to manifest). The kind of karmic load of the 5 of Cups is one of the hardest of all the types of karmic consequences to surmount - Dridha karma. Biden would've needed to draw upon higher spiritual plane forces to make it a success but people who know how to do that typically don't choose administrative, horse-trading, hob-knobbing, political careers imo. Politicians tend to be material-plane power-focused individuals. It's unusual these days to find politicians capable and willing to use higher plane energies to overturn earthly manifestation trends. That's probably for the best imo.

2. Factor feeding into the Heart:

The Sun R

Optimism fades, darkening clouds, troubles ahead.


Karma Type: All types
Karma Quality: All types

A 90 degree card reinforcing the Heart card. So this is indicating an even bigger intensification than the already large negative intensification of the 5 of Cups R itself. This is indicating Biden is most likely feeling very down in the dumps today though he probably won't show it publicly or to his staff.

3. Unconscious Factor underpinning the first two:

Strength R

Reading: Weakness Revealed.

The support needed for the first two factors to be a success is entirely absent. Failure to close the deal. Unconvincing speech.


Too many people are wary about unintended, detrimental consequences of BBB. It's also made clear to everyone - friend and foe - just how weak a President Biden is. Prior to this there might have been willingness to be charitable. But since his horse-trading with his own fellow Dem didn't seal the deal everyone now knows he's a hamstrung President even to his own party - the very people who were supposed to have his back. For him to be a success now he'll likely have to give away too much in negotiations and at that point the people in greatest power within the Democratic Party are asking, "What do we gain by a President relinquishing so much of what we want to do?"


The answer this card gives is: Nothing. Total Loss of Capable Action. He just exposed their Party inter-rivalry weaknesses to all and sundry. There's now a much improved chance to bypass Biden and target powerful Dems to peel away support from the President's legislative agendas. Something that out-of-favor groups will be sure to exploit to make an end-run around him and his supporters. Especially in upcoming mid-term elections.

Karma type: Parabdha and Purushartha
Karma quality: Dridha - fixed, immutable, unmovable

4. Waning Factor:

2 of Cups

Reading: harmony between parties, pleasure, friendships, giving and receiving gifts.

Card indicating mutually beneficial partnerships. This shakti is in ebb.


Karma type: Parabdha and Purushartha
Karma quality: Dridha - fixed, immutable

Outcome from the Above: The Lovers R

Reading (the following is from Sri Arya's tarot book): Hard choices, inability to hold or nurture healthy relationships, suspicion, mistrust, blind to inner contradictions or conflicts to the point of hypocrisy, using people as a medium for pleasure. An inability to see other people's point of view.

My note:

Which is weird because I've gotten this card in relation to Biden in some other readings too. Very odd since the press says he's renown for sticking up for the 'average' citizen in remembrance of the 'humble roots' he came from. This card often turning up makes me think Biden is seriously fooling himself when he keeps re-iterating he hasn't been changed by all the wealth and power he's steeped himself in for most of his adult life in D.C. He's been there since 1973! He's been steeped in the D.C. halls of wealth and power continuously since 1973. That's longer than many people reading this blog have been alive. That's worthy of the old Soviet Politburo. He really may be the U.S.'s Yuri Andropov. What he thinks of as an 'average' citizen and what an actual 'average' (especially non-PMC) citizen of today is are seriously mismatched in his head imo if I understand this card correctly. What that means in terms of his Presidential legislative agendas I don't know. Probably more taxpayer boondoggles if I had to speculate.

Karma type: Parabdha and Purushartha
and Agama
Karma quality: Dridha - fixed, immobile, immovable

Agama karmas are those action-tendencies we accumulate (due the interaction of Parabdha and Purushartha) that do not come to fruition. The quality of this card is saying the seed of The Lovers R is strong enough it will sprout again someday to try to get fulfilled in order to clear itself out. That is, since it's on the unfulfilled-balance side of the scale in Biden it's destiny is to manifest again someday to balance the shakti-ledger.


I quote the 5th card info directly from Sri Arya's book since it seems to be saying some rather un-nerving things about Biden himself imo:

I turned over the following card:


5. Waxing Factor:

The Moon R

Reading: delusion, mental fog, confusion, inability to properly assess proper and pleasant courses of action, mood swings, alternating rage and charm. Dangerously persuasive mode of speech prior to deception or swindling (my note: ie, being very good at running a long con). Sri Arya said this in regard to The Moon R: "These people can conceal personal problems and addictions, with nobody having a clue, until it all blows up in everybody's face." He also said it indicates a tendency toward refusing to consider the consequences of what transpires so long as things go well for the time being. This is the mentality of a corporate executive team that flushes long-term well-being of a business down the toilet because they all gain from the decisions made in the short-term. I'm not surprised if this same mentality turns up in government officials too.

Karma type: all types
Karma quality: Dridha-Adridha (some of it fixed and some of it very fluid)

I leave it to the readership to draw their own conclusions about what the 5th card - especially in relation to the other 4 - might mean for the rest of the Biden term of office.

****

Thus sayeth the Mint Puckish Goose

comments.title: simple solstice ritual
comments.content:
Hello,

I was wondering if you or a member of this community could recommend a simple ritual that would be suitable for one person to celebrate the solstice. Perhaps something that is as agnostic re. faith or tradition as the Sphere of Protection ritual that is described on this Dreamwidth blog, or if it belongs to a particular faith or tradition, one that is welcoming of a well-meaning guest. Thank you.

replies.title: Re: simple solstice ritual
replies.content: Set aside a quarter hour or so after sunset, and have a candle, matches, and a chair, as well as food and drink for afterwards. Turn down the lights. Sit down, and think about the season; reflect on other winters you've known, and on the year that's ending. Wait until it feels right, and then light the candle. Thank the year that's ending and welcome the year that's beginning -- do it out loud. Then eat and drink in celebration. That's a very simple Druid winter solstice ceremony; I hope it works for you.

comments.content:

There appears to be a strong theme of Storm gods fighting serpentine creature's Marduk and Tiamat (Mesopotamia), Susanoo and Orochi (Japan), He-no and a horned serpent whose name eludes me (Iroquois), Thor and Jormungandr (Sandinavia), and Perun and Veles (Slavic) why do you feel that there is this connection between Storm Deities and Snake/dragon like creatures across several different cultures

replies.title: Re: Serpentine Creature's V. Storm Gods
replies.content:
1) The serpent is the classic symbol of the telluric or terrestrial current; the thunder god is the classic symbol of the solar or celestial current. Those two currents interact in complex ways to create fertility. The thunder god carries a hammer because driving a stake into the ground is the prime geomantic act, the process from which every sacred place that unites the two currents is founded. This is a very ancient magic and it's found over most of the world.

2) It's just out -- I'll be making an announcement shortly.
